The Anchorage
Agentic Launch Sprint
00Overview 01Phase map 02Launch Calendar 03Subsprints SS-1Research Paper SS-2Livestream SS-3Nathan's Video & Talking Points SS-4X Article 04Content library 05Internal livestream 06Conversion 07Action items
Agentic Launch Sprint · Ready for flight (built, not yet launched) · Confidential

Who banks
the agents?

Campaign positioning line
Agentic accountability starts with an anchor.
The through-line for the whole sprint: agents act, value moves, and someone is accountable for it. Anchorage is the regulated anchor that makes that accountability real.

A two-week, multi-voice launch that makes Anchorage the name attached to one question: agents are becoming economic actors, and what lets them act is regulated financial rails. The whole sprint argues that banking an agent is a controls problem (segmented accounts, spending limits, Know Your Agent, a human on the hook), not an AI problem.

2 weeks · Mon-Fri x2 Week 1 Jun 24-28 Week 2 Jul 1 - Jul 5 Launch day T-0 = Mon Jun 29 Funnel end → Agentic Banking waitlist
Logistics master (Google Sheet) Action items (7 topics)

Read it top to bottom: a belief-anchored phase map (Hype to Launch to Convert), then the Launch Calendar as a wide grid that scrolls right, the six subsprints (SS-1 to SS-4) that feed the funnel, a content library of the live Google Docs, the internal-livestream rationale, the conversion rationale, and the action items. Every working doc linked here is a live Google Doc or Sheet, open for comment. Engagement figures across this build are illustrative projections, not performance claims.

01

Phase map

The sprint as three belief-anchored stages. Each starts from a Nathan belief (the why), carries it with named assets, and ships with a checklist of deliverables to confirm before that stage is met. Hype creates the attention, Launch earns the press, Convert routes everything to one waitlist.

01HYPET-3 to T-1 (Jun 24-26)
Belief anchor
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
Agents are becoming economic actors, and no one has focused on who banks them.
Start from the question the whole agent economy skipped. Ask it out loud as a credible institution, give it a name (Know Your Agent), and let breadcrumbs and a raw reveal build a staked position before any product is shown. This sprint owns agentic banking specifically, not broad AI. Hype creates the attention the launch will convert.
Assets
Breadcrumb tweets (WHO BANKS THE AGENTS, KYA)Nathan selfie vision video (bullet talking points, his words)Research paper: Who Banks the AgentsX article bridging the paper and the tweetsControls thread (allowance not your wallet, the 3am test)KOL amplification
Deliverables to confirm
→
02LAUNCHT-2 to T-0 (Jun 25-29)
Belief anchor
S4
SECURITY-FIRSTSecurity is the founding discipline and is never finished. Banking an agent is a controls problem before it is an AI problem.
Build the controls first, inside the regulatory perimeter, then let the agent act.
The launch earns attention because the hard part is real: segmented accounts, spending limits, verified merchants, KYA, a human on the hook. Put Nathan on a live, moderated stage. The charter is the part a competitor cannot copy with a funding round. This is an institution-only launch.
Assets
Launch announcement (institution-only)Press coverage (media set: business, tech, crypto)Nathan livestream (Riverside, moderated)Agentic banking landing page (become a design partner)
Deliverables to confirm
→
03CONVERTT-0 to T+7 (Jun 29 - Jul 8)
Belief anchor
S26
PLURALITY / ANTI-MONOPOLYCentralizing on one entity betrays the nature of the thing. Build the category with a coalition, in the open, not under one logo.
We would rather build this in the open, with the builders, than behind a curtain.
Resolve the arc into one institutional on-ramp: become a design partner. Anchorage is institution-only, so the ask is a design-partner conversation with the enterprises building where agents meet money, not a consumer waitlist. Builder prompts and the Victor AMA turn replies into a demand roadmap, all while attention is still doing the work. Everything routes to one page and one institutional ask.
Assets
Become a design partner (institutional CTA)X article (repurposed Consensus talk)Builder promptsVictor builder AMA (confirmed, T+6)Coalition close + Sprint 2 forward-reference
Deliverables to confirm
End state: every breadcrumb, paper post, and livestream moment routes to the agentic landing page and the Agentic Banking waitlist. The waitlist is the single conversion event the whole sprint is built to produce.
02

Launch Calendar

The three-week run (one prep week plus two execution weeks) as a wide grid that scrolls right. Items are bundled by topic and connection, not sorted by owner, and slotted in the order they go out: first, second, third, with spread-out times. Each day opens with its Narrative and two supporting subs, carries a T-minus label (T-0 = Mon Jun 29), and shows its tweet threads inline: a Nathan tweet with its KOL quote-tweets and comments nested directly under it. The livestream and the paper drop carry their own associative tweets on their days. The belief footnote on each card is clickable: it reveals the belief and why the post aligns. Owners ride as tags inside each bundle. Engagement is illustrative.

Tweets master, open for edits and comments
Every tweet, time, owner, and handle in one organized Google Doc, laid out as a table. Suggest edits or drop a comment in line. This is the working copy for the calendar below.
Open the Tweets master doc
All tweets, master (Google Doc)
TEASE / REVEAL
RESEARCH
PARTNER
PROOF
PR
LAUNCH
»»» scrolls right · 10 days
Mon Jun 22
T-5PREP
Prep week: get the paper, the video, and the page production-ready before any post.
Sub-1: Paper draft goes to counsel; Nathan gets the video talking points to put in his own words.
Sub-2: The design-partner landing page moves into production.
Prep week opens
PREPPAPER
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
Production starts. The research paper draft goes to counsel for review (about one week of lead time). Nathan gets the video talking points to put in his own words. The design-partner landing page moves into production. Nothing posts yet.
Lead time: kick prep 1 to 2 weeks ahead; Nathan sign-off window opens.
Tue Jun 23
T-4PREP
Counsel review runs while the institutional landing page is wired and tracked.
Sub-1: Lead times are real: the paper alone needs about a week of review and graphics.
Sub-2: The page carries a become-a-design-partner call to action, institution-only.
Page + counsel
PREPBRAND
S2
REGULATION-AS-CHOSEN-PATHAnchorage sought the highest regulatory scrutiny on purpose. The charter is the moat a competitor cannot copy with a funding round.
Institutional landing page wired. The agentic banking page is built and tracked, carrying a become a design partner call to action. Anchorage is institution-only, so the ask is a design-partner conversation, not a consumer waitlist. Counsel review of the paper continues.
Lead time: counsel review about 1 week; product sign-off on the page.
Wed Jun 24
T-3TEASE
No one has focused on who banks the agents.
Sub-1: Anchorage is leading the charge, bringing agentic banking to enterprise on regulated rails.
Sub-2: Breadcrumbs hint that Anchorage is launching something, without naming it yet.

TWEETNATHAN
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.

INSTITUTIONAL-ADOPTIONThe institutions were always here; latent demand just gets met. The agent account is the next account in that lineage.
push 12:11p ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
Agents can already advise you.

Imagine a world where they can earn/spend money, and even act autonomously.

This is the start of the agentic evolution into an economic actor, and economic actors cannot be fully realized while being unbanked.
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Crypto KOL 1
@anchor_crypto_kol1
KOLQT
The agents are coming stopped being sci-fi a while ago. It's a roadmap line item now. The banking part is the gap, and almost nobody is building for it.

QT: agents can advise today. Acting is the part that needs money, and money needs rails.
Thu Jun 25
T-2REVEAL
Trusting an agent with money is a new bar, so it needs a new check: Know Your Agent.
Sub-1: KYC and KYB taught us to vet people and companies; KYA is the obvious next leg.
Sub-2: Nathan's raw selfie video resolves the breadcrumbs into a committed direction.
KYA breadcrumb
BREADCRUMBNATHAN
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
S2

push 8:52a ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
KYA.

KNOW YOUR AGENT.
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Compliance KOL 1
@anchor_compliance_kol1
KOLQT
KYA. Of course. We KYC humans, we KYB businesses, and an autonomous actor that holds and moves money is the missing third leg. Cleanest framing of the next compliance decade I've seen.

QT: KYA. Know Your Agent.
AK
Anchor Compliance KOL 2
@anchor_compliance_kol2
KOLCOMMENT
Every compliance team is going to seize on this acronym. It maps a brand-new problem onto language we already trust and already audit against. That's how a control actually gets adopted.
TWEETNATHAN
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
S15
PLATFORM / BANK-FOR-BANKSThe enabling infrastructure other banks build on. Agentic banking is the rails layer the category needs.
push 1:05p ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
Rails are our prohibitor to technological acceleration.

Not Models.
#ReBank
Google Doc
AK
Anchor AI KOL 1
@anchor_ai_kol1
KOLCOMMENT
As a model person it stings a little to admit, but he's right. The capability curve is not the binding constraint anymore. The agent can reason fine. It can't transact safely. That's a rails problem and it lives outside the model.

VIDEONATHANSS-3
S24

push Thu 6:30p ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
Raw selfie to camera, shot on his phone. The breadcrumbs resolve into a committed direction: Anchorage is going all in on agentic banking. Same crux problem as custody, one layer out. We are building the bridge.
▶ Reveal video poster
Bullet talking points in SS-4, not a script. Requires heavy editing.
Video talking points (Google Doc)
AK
Anchor VC KOL 1
@anchor_vc_kol1
KOLQT
Recorded on his phone, no script, no end card. That is the most credible way to announce something this big. You only skip the production when the substance carries it. Builders notice that.

QT: [Nathan reveal video]
AK
Anchor Builder KOL 1
@anchor_builder_kol1
KOLCOMMENT
The line that got me: it's the same crux problem, one layer out. Custody was how do you hold a bearer asset safely. This is how do you let an agent move money safely. Same instinct, new actor.
Fri Jun 26
T-1PRIME
The campaign line goes up and the breadcrumbs resolve the day before launch, priming the audience for the answer.
Sub-1: The thesis is fully in market; the audience knows the question and is waiting on who banks the agents.
Sub-2: The campaign line sets position ahead of launch; the page goes live tomorrow.
Campaign banner
Agentic accountability starts with an anchor.
The campaign carries one line above a defensible, factual headline. Anchorage is leading the charge of agentic banking for institutions: the regulated place an institution's agent holds and moves value. The headline below stays factual; the banner sets the position. Tagline is a changeable media hook.
QTNATHAN
S2
REGULATION-AS-CHOSEN-PATHAnchorage sought the highest regulatory scrutiny on purpose. The charter is the moat a competitor cannot copy with a funding round.
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
push 7:10a ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
The charter is the part you cannot raise your way into. We chose the highest scrutiny on purpose.
QT: illustrative coverage, Anchorage launches agentic banking for institutions.
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Fin KOL 1
@anchor_fin_kol1
KOLQT
First and only fully operational federally chartered digital asset bank, now standing up agentic banking. That clause is the entire moat. Competitors can copy the product page. They cannot copy the charter with a funding round.

QT: @CoinDesk, Anchorage becomes the first regulated agentic bank.
AK
Anchor Compliance KOL 1
@anchor_compliance_kol1
KOLCOMMENT
The understatement is the flex. Most teams would be screaming. He's pointing at the consent-order-terminated, inside-the-perimeter part, because that's the part that actually de-risks an agent moving money. Right instinct.
Mon Jun 29
T-0LAUNCH
Launch day: the agentic banking page is live and the announcement lands.
Sub-1: Not a better model: a regulated place for an institution's agent to hold and spend under rules, KYA at the core.
Sub-2: The ask is to become a design partner. A center point and proof-of-work flag, not a hard conversion event.

push 1:30p ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
If your agent could have its own regulated bank account and spend within limits, what would you build?
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Builder KOL 1
@anchor_builder_kol1
KOLCOMMENT
An ops agent that reconciles and pays our vendors overnight, capped per-vendor, with every payment leaving a trail I can audit in the morning. I've wanted to build that for a year and the missing piece was always a real account with limits. Joining.
AK
Anchor AI KOL 1
@anchor_ai_kol1
KOLQT
Direct builder prompts like this generate the best replies on the timeline. The thread under it is going to read like a demand roadmap for the whole category. Smart way to co-design in public.

QT: if your agent had its own balance and limits, what would you build?

push 1:15p ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
Once you frame it right, the interesting problem is not the AI at all. It is a compliance and controls problem, the kind banks have worked on for a hundred years.

If you are building where agents meet money, come find us.
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Fin KOL 2
@anchor_fin_kol2
KOLQT
You would not hand a new hire the master account on day one. An allowance with limits is the obvious parallel for agents, and it makes spend controls intuitive for people who never thought about agent risk before.

QT: give it an allowance, not your account.
AK
Anchor Risk KOL 2
@anchor_risk_kol2
KOLCOMMENT
I ran bank treasury for years and was a hard skeptic on agents touching money. This is the first framing that flips me. An allowance is a real control. A capped wallet bounds the loss but doesn't govern the behavior. Governance is the part that's worth paying for.
Thu Jul 2
T+3PROOF
Banking an agent is the boring, hard part, and boring is the moat.
Sub-1: Give an agent an allowance, not your account.
Sub-2: When an agent pays the wrong party at 3am, only an accountable bank can pick up.
The 3am test
TWEETNATHAN
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
S4
SECURITY-FIRSTSecurity is the founding discipline and is never finished. Banking an agent is a controls problem before it is an AI problem.
push 9:15a ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
When your agent pays the wrong party at 3am, who picks up the phone, and what can they actually do about it?

If the honest answer is no one, you do not have a bank for agents.
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Risk KOL 1
@anchor_risk_kol1
KOLQT
The 3am test is the sharpest articulation of agent-banking risk I've seen. Capability and trust are not the same axis. If no one picks up the phone, no serious money flows. Accountability is the precondition for scale, full stop.

QT: when your agent pays the wrong party at 3am, who picks up?
Controls thread
TWEETNATHAN
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
S4
SECURITY-FIRSTSecurity is the founding discipline and is never finished. Banking an agent is a controls problem before it is an AI problem.
push 12:15p ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
You do not hand an agent your whole account. You give it an allowance. A segmented account with spending limits and a verified merchant on the other side. Scoped permissions, like you would give a new hire.
Google Doc
AK
Anchor Builder KOL 2
@anchor_builder_kol2
KOLQT
Segmented accounts and spend limits are boring, and that's precisely why they matter. The exciting layer is commoditizing. The boring layer is the moat, and almost nobody is doing it inside a charter.

QT: a bank for agents is a controls problem, not an AI problem.
AK
Anchor Compliance KOL 2
@anchor_compliance_kol2
KOLCOMMENT
Verified merchants plus human-attached liability is the line. A control that doesn't name who's accountable isn't a control, it's a hope. This names it.

03

Subsprints

The four supporting tracks that feed the funnel, each labeled and shown with its tweet thread inline (tweet, quote-tweet, comment) and one Google Doc at the top of the block. They live under the Launch Calendar and roll up into the days above.

SS-1

Research paper, "Who Banks the Agents"

T+1
The credibility engine of the sprint and the source every paper-phase post is chopped from. It drops as a thread: the brand handle announces it with a clean, clickable paper thumbnail, and Nathan quote-tweets. One Google Doc at the top; the paper is not linked on every tweet. The placement question (Research page vs Agentic Banking page) is an open call for Anchorage.
Placement · where the paper lives (open question for Anchorage)
Proposed launch date: Monday, June 29 (T-0). The paper is a research publication, not a press release, and wants a durable, citable home. Two options are on the table for Anchorage to decide: the Research page of anchorage.com, or the Agentic Banking page, cross-linked either way so the product narrative points back to the thesis. Every tweet drives to that one post, not to a one-off link.
Production lead time · about one week
The paper alone needs roughly a week: draft, then counsel review, then graphics, before any post. Counsel clears the KYA and human-attached-liability language for external use. Two opener framings (V1 and V2) sit side by side for Anchorage to weigh against each other; the V2 charter-race opener may read as premature. Competitor names are cut.

SS-2

Livestream

T+4
Two live moments, both Nathan and the Anchorage AI team. The Nathan × Victor livestream (announced T-2, airs T+4) is a moderated conversation between Nathan and Victor, a member of Anchorage's own agentic-engineering team, on who banks the agents and what it takes to build agentic banking. The Victor builder AMA (confirmed, T+6) turns the design-partner questions into a live roadmap. Both run this sprint on Riverside, moderated, structured for a high-success first outing. Showing the team behind the product is more credible and more controllable than waiting on an outside partner.

SS-3

Nathan's video & talking points

T-2
The reveal. A raw, front-facing selfie shot on his phone, no end card, where the breadcrumbs resolve into a committed direction. Raw outperforms polished here because the substance carries it. We give Nathan bullet talking points, not a script; he puts them in his own words, can one-shot it, and likes about five takes. Needs about 15 to 20 minutes to film, and the cut requires heavy editing.

QT: [Nathan reveal video]
AK
Anchor Builder KOL 1
@anchor_builder_kol1
KOLCOMMENT
The line that got me: it's the same crux problem, one layer out. Custody was how do you hold a bearer asset safely. This is how do you let an agent move money safely. Same instinct, new actor.
Agentic Banking, Nathan McCauley
Hero video · raw selfie, front-facing · nine talking points, his own words
Draft · Requires heavy editing
Open it himself
No script, no slides. Today is the start of a new frontier for Anchorage.
Agents are economic actors
Right now most agents can advise you. What they cannot do yet is act: hold money, get paid, be told no.
Rails, not a better model
What lets an agent act is not a better model. It is financial rails. That is what we are going all in on. We call it agentic banking.
Same crux problem, one layer out
This is the same crux problem we solved with custody, one layer out. We are building the bridge between autonomous software and regulated money.
Who banks the agents
When an agent pays the wrong party at 3am, who picks up the phone, and what can they actually do about it?
A controls problem
Most people think this is an AI problem. It is mostly a controls problem. You give an agent an allowance, not your whole account, with spending limits, verified merchants, and a human still on the hook.
KYA, Know Your Agent
Banks already do know your customer and know your business. We take it one step further: KYA. We underwrite the agent itself, its history and identity and what it is allowed to do.
An institutional bank that chose scrutiny
We are an institutional bank, and we sought the highest level of scrutiny on purpose. We spent years inside the regulatory perimeter building exactly these boring, hard controls.
Built in the open; the ask
One company should not hold the whole thing. We would rather build it in the open and bring institutions onto these rails with us. We are putting our thinking out this week. If you build where agents meet money, come find us.
Producer notes. These are talking points, not lines to read. Keep it raw and in his voice. He can one-shot it or run up to five takes; stumbles read as human and build trust. No music, no lower-thirds, no end card on the social cut. End on one clear call to action: become a design partner on the agentic banking page. No em dashes spoken or on screen.

Ready replies, when people ask "what is KYA?"

KYA is Know Your Agent. We already do Know Your Customer and Know Your Business. KYA is the third leg: you underwrite the agent itself, its history, its identity, and what it is actually allowed to do, not just the human who owns it.
ready reply
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
S2
REGULATION-AS-CHOSEN-PATHAnchorage sought the highest regulatory scrutiny on purpose. The charter is the moat a competitor cannot copy with a funding round.
A capped wallet bounds the loss. It does not govern the behavior. An allowance with a verified counterparty and a human on the hook is a control. That difference is the whole product.
ready reply
S4
SECURITY-FIRSTSecurity is the founding discipline and is never finished. Banking an agent is a controls problem before it is an AI problem.
S5
BUILDER-NOT-SPECULATORNathan is the engineer who solved a concrete problem, not a speculator. The reveal is raw because the substance carries it.
SS-4

X article, "Who Banks the Agents"

T+1
A short long-form X article that bridges the research paper and the breadcrumb tweets. Repurposed from Nathan's Consensus talk, in his own voice: why agentic banking is a thing, and why we believe in it. Easier to finish quickly than the full paper, and a strong standalone item. Posts from Nathan's account at T+1, amplified by the brand at T+2.
Why this piece · the bridge
The paper is the deep reference; the tweets are the breadcrumbs. This article sits in between: long enough to carry the thesis, short enough to read in one sitting and to ship before the paper clears. It is the on-ramp from a one-line breadcrumb to the full argument, and it drives to the same place.
X article (Google Doc)
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley · long-form on X
499 words · target 450 to 550

Who banks the agents?

For a while now I have been saying the same thing in every room I am in. Agents are going to be economic actors. Right now, in most use cases, they advise and inform. What they still cannot do is act. Hold money. Get paid. Be told no. And what truly lets an agent act is not a better model. It is financial rails.

That is the whole idea behind what we are calling Agentic Banking. It is the bridge between two worlds: a system that brings trust, governance, and real financial rails to autonomous software. Once you frame it that way, the interesting problem is not the AI at all. It is a compliance and controls problem, the kind banks have worked on for a hundred years.

Start with the obvious question. When your agent pays the wrong party at three in the morning, who picks up the phone, and what can they actually do about it? If the honest answer is no one, you do not have a bank for an agent. You have an agent with a card and crossed fingers.

So the work is the boring part. You do not hand an agent your whole account. You give it a segmented one, an allowance, with spending limits and verified merchants on the other side, and a human who is still on the hook for it. Bank compliance already does KYC and KYB, know your customer and know your business. We are taking it a step further to KYA, know your agent. We do not just underwrite the human who owns the bot. We underwrite the agent itself: its history, its identity, and a shared understanding of what it is and what it is allowed to do.

None of this is a coincidence for us. The crux problem we solved to build Anchorage was custody, holding a bearer asset securely and still being able to do useful things with it. Banking an agent is the same crux problem, one layer out. We spent years inside the regulatory perimeter building exactly those controls, and we sought the highest level of scrutiny on purpose, because regulation and innovation are not mutually exclusive.

I do not think this should be one company holding the whole thing. That would be a little anathema to why any of this got started. The industry is better when it is built in the open, by a lot of people. We would rather welcome everyone into it and be the rails underneath.

This is why we are publishing our thinking now, ahead of any product. The attention is real, the question is open, and we would rather answer it in public than wait. If you are an institution building where agents meet money, we want to hear from you.

Who banks the agents? Less about the AI than people expect. Mostly about controls, identity, and who is accountable when an agent moves money. That is the work. We are glad to be doing it.

Belief-checked. Drafted in Nathan's voice and run against his belief record: every claim is consistent with what he has said publicly (agentic banking, the bridge, KYA, regulation-as-chosen-path, build-in-the-open). The KYA and accountability lines route through counsel with the paper. No em dashes. His to amend before posting.
X ARTICLENATHANSS-4
S24
AGENTIC-BANKINGAI agents are becoming economic actors and need trust, governance, and rails. The whole sprint is built on this.
S31
BRIDGE-BETWEEN-TWO-WORLDSAnchorage is the connective layer between two worlds. It bridges agents and regulated money.
T+1, 11:30a ET
NM
Nathan McCauley
@nathanmccauley
Who banks the agents? I wrote up why we believe agentic banking is a thing, repurposed from my Consensus talk. The short version: what lets an agent act is not a better model, it is financial rails. Full article below.
X article (Google Doc)

The bottleneck was never the model, it was the money layer. Writing it down made that obvious in a way a year of threads didn't. This reframes the whole agent-stack conversation. Reading tonight.

05

Why an internal AI-team livestream

Nathan in conversation with his own team, this sprint. The launch does not wait on an outside partner. The T+4 livestream is Nathan and Victor, from Anchorage's agentic-engineering team, talking through who banks the agents and what it takes to build agentic banking. Showing the people who actually build the product is more credible and more controllable than a borrowed co-sign: the thesis comes from the bank that is shipping the rails, in the voices of the team shipping them.

Nathan × Victor livestream brief (Google Doc)
Why this supports the sprint
The whole sprint argues one thing: agents are becoming economic actors, and what lets them act is regulated financial rails, not a smarter model. That argument lands hardest when it comes from the people building the rails. Nathan sets the thesis; Victor shows the engineering reality underneath it. The market reads a chartered bank that is actually shipping this, not a marketing claim.

The strategic advantage

Built-in credibility
A working agentic-engineering team talking through the hard parts, on the record, turns a brand claim into proof of work. The substance is the validation.
Fully controllable
No outside calendar, no approval chain on someone else's copy, no waiting. The conversation runs this sprint on Anchorage's own terms and timeline.
The team is the story
Victor can tell the story Nathan cannot tell alone: what it actually takes to give an agent an account, controls, and accountability. Seeing the builders makes the launch feel real, not aspirational.
Genuine, not staged
Because both speak from inside the build, the conversation reads as real. Anchorage owns the controls, the charter, and the engineering, and says so in its own voices.

What the livestream asks of the AI team

  1. Carry the substance. Victor walks the real mechanics of the agent account live: identity, controls, and who is accountable when an agent moves money. Nathan frames the thesis around it.
  2. Speak from the build. The team talks in its own voice about what shipping agentic banking actually takes. No borrowed lineage, just the people doing the work.
  3. Sit for the conversation. A roughly 25 to 30 minute live conversation between Nathan and Victor on the agent economy and regulated rails, on Riverside, with a neutral moderator who keeps it substantive. Airs T+4.
  4. Feed the roadmap. The questions design partners are asking shape the follow-on builder AMA (Victor, T+6), so the live moments compound into a demand roadmap.
Why the team carries the paper
The thesis is strongest when the people who built the rails put their name on it. The livestream is the live companion to "Who Banks the Agents": Nathan and Victor talk through the same argument the paper makes, in the open, so the substance is demonstrated and not just asserted. It is light by design: a real conversation, structured for a high-success first outing, fully inside Anchorage's control.
@nathanmccauley CEOVictor agentic-engineering team
06

Conversion

The conversion mechanic for this launch is a design-partner application, not a consumer waitlist. This sprint is institution-only.

Anchorage is a regulated, institution-facing bank, and the first real users of agentic banking are institutions, not retail consumers. So the launch routes every thread, the paper, the press, and the livestream to one place: an application to become an agentic-banking design partner. That ask qualifies serious intent, opens a real relationship with the institutions who will shape the product, and keeps the whole motion inside the regulatory perimeter where Anchorage operates. A broad consumer waitlist belongs to a later, retail chapter, not to Sprint 1.
Design-partner application · the ask
Invites a qualified institution to raise its hand and start a conversation. It captures serious, named intent at peak attention and begins the design-partner relationships that build the product. The right instrument for an institution-first launch.
Consumer waitlist · not this sprint
A broad consumer signup is a retail instrument for a later chapter. It collects unqualified volume and points at a consumer product this sprint is not announcing. Deferred, not the ask here.
Become an agentic-banking design partner.

Why design partners, and how we will know it worked

On an institution-first, pre-product launch, the read is the quality and seriousness of applications, not raw signup volume. Here is what we measure and why an institutional ask is the right one. Figures below are illustrative, industry-typical ranges, not promises.

What a design-partner motion signals · illustrative
An institution-facing launch on a credible thesis and a named founder converts qualified institutional interest, not retail headcount. A handful of serious design-partner conversations from the right institutions is a stronger early signal than thousands of cold consumer signups, because each one is a named relationship with an organization that can actually deploy. These are reference framings, used to size the motion, not to forecast a result.
What we measure
Qualified applications (institutions, not individuals), application velocity in the 72 hours after launch (the cleanest read on whether the thesis lands with institutions), declared use cases from the applications, and the pipeline of design-partner conversations the team carries forward. Every number ties to a tracked landing page.
Why institution-first now
The attention is real now, while Anchorage owns the question no competitor has claimed. An institution-first ask converts that attention into real, named relationships with the organizations that will actually build on the rails, rather than a cold consumer list. The retail chapter, if it comes, is later and broader.
Read for Kate: the conversion ask is an institutional design-partner application, not a consumer waitlist, with a defined success read (qualified application volume and 72-hour velocity against the illustrative ranges above). If the velocity read is weak, that is itself a cheap, early signal before any product spend.
07

Seven topic groups, each with dependency-numbered sub-items. Owners ride as tags; due dates are T-minus labels (T-0 = Mon Jun 29). Confirm owners against the real team before this is binding.

The Anchorage Agentic Launch Sprint · v8 · Winn.solutions · confidential review build. Unlisted and noindexed. KOL personas are fictional and illustrative. Partner copy is suggested and requires approval. Engagement numbers and mockups are illustrative projections, not performance claims. No copy here is cleared for outbound use without review.
